location.name: Spirewick, Cornwall
location.weather: iron-grey skies, sea-mist clinging to the rocks

crime.time_of_death: between ten and eleven o'clock at night
crime.motive: preventing exposure of a false identity

characters.name: Aldous Crayne
characters.description: A well-dressed man in his mid-fifties, a travelling antiquarian who had lodged in Spirewick for three weeks. Colleagues described him as meticulous and private. The body lies slumped against the salt-store's stone wall, one arm thrown outward, his coat still buttoned against the cold. A dark stain has spread across the limestone behind his head.

characters.name: Edmund Voss
characters.description: The Harbourmaster of Spirewick, a broad-shouldered man of forty-one with close-cropped grey-brown hair and an air of steady authority. He walks with a slight hitch on his right side. When the investigator meets him, he is studying tide-charts at his desk, and he offers a firm, dry handshake with his left hand, his right sleeve pushed up to reveal a pale burn scar along the forearm.

characters.name: Nessa Tallow
characters.description: A weather-beaten fishwife of thirty-eight who found the body during her dawn walk to check her family's crab pots near the salt-store. She is still visibly shaken, her hands wrapped around a cold mug of tea, and she speaks in short, precise sentences. She meets the investigator's eye only briefly.

characters.name: Miriam Quist
characters.description: A widow of sixty-seven, slight and sharp-featured, who lives alone in a stone cottage at the foot of the headland lane. She is alert and precise in her speech, though she volunteers little unprompted. She receives the investigator at her door wrapped in a shawl, one eye slightly red, as if from sleeplessness.

characters.name: Dorothea Penrose
characters.description: The lighthouse keeper's daughter, thirty-one, who has run the lighthouse alone since her father's death two years ago. She is composed and capable, with the distant manner of someone accustomed to solitude. She speaks warmly of Edmund Voss but chooses her words carefully when the investigator asks about recent weeks.

characters.name: Reverend Silas Crowe
characters.description: The village vicar, fifty-eight, a lean man with deep-set eyes and an unhurried way of speaking that suggests long practice at pastoral listening. He meets the investigator in the church porch and is courteous but measured, his gaze thoughtful rather than evasive.

characters.name: Pasco Tregidden
characters.description: The deputy harbourmaster, twenty-nine, lean and sharp-faced with ink-stained fingers and restless eyes. He is openly unhappy about the investigator's presence and answers questions in short, deflecting bursts, often checking over his shoulder toward the fee ledgers on the shelf behind him.

items.name: Iron Mooring Pin
items.description: A heavy octagonal iron pin about fourteen inches long, found on the ground near the body; one end is darkly stained and the surface bears a faint stamped mark near the base.

items.name: Harbour Authority Stamp Register
items.description: A bound ledger kept in the harbour office recording all equipment issued under the Spirewick harbour authority mark, with columns for item type, date issued, and current location.

items.name: Crayne's Room Key
items.description: A plain iron room key on a wooden fob marked with the number 4, recovered from the victim's coat pocket.

items.name: Empty Leather Notebook Case
items.description: A slim leather slip-case sized for a small notebook, found open and empty on the writing desk in room four of the inn; the interior shows wear marks consistent with regular use.

items.name: Storm Lantern
items.description: A large brass storm lantern of a type used by harbour and dock workers, found hanging on its usual hook inside the harbour office door.

locations.name: The Anchor Inn
locations.flavour: Low-beamed and salt-warped, the inn smells of tallow candles and old wool; the landlord keeps a coal fire burning even when no guests are down.

locations.name: Harbour Office
locations.flavour: A squat stone building overlooking the quay, its windows perpetually fogged with condensation, charts pinned in overlapping layers across every wall.

locations.name: Headland Salt-Store
locations.flavour: An ancient low building of dressed limestone, half-buried in the headland slope, where barrels of cured catch have been stacked since before anyone in the village can remember.

locations.name: Quist Cottage
locations.flavour: A single-storey cottage hunched at the lane's foot, its one seaward window facing directly up the headland path, curtains never quite closed.

locations.name: Spirewick Lighthouse
locations.flavour: The easternmost point of the headland, where the limestone spire is at its narrowest and the wind off the sea is a constant low roar.

locations.name: St Warna's Church
locations.flavour: A small Norman church set back from the sea, its graveyard full of fishermen's headstones worn smooth by two centuries of salt wind.

locations.name: Headland Path
locations.flavour: A narrow track of compacted chalk and shingle running along the clifftop, exposed on both sides to the sea-wind and visible from the cottage windows below.

checkpoints.label: Where did the murder take place?
When did Aldous Crayne die?
answer_options: between ten and eleven o'clock at night
between eight and nine o'clock at night
between six and seven o'clock in the evening
between midnight and one o'clock in the morning
between two and three o'clock in the morning

checkpoints.label: Why was Aldous Crayne killed?
answer_options: preventing exposure of a false identity
silencing a blackmail victim who threatened to confess
protecting a family secret about inheritance

clues.text: Examining the body at [loc:salt_store], the investigator finds the back of the skull deeply indented, the wound edges irregular and compressed. The limestone wall behind [char:Aldous Crayne]'s head carries a fan-shaped dark smear at approximately seated height.

The stained end of the iron mooring pin is heavily dented and carries matted dark fibres caught in the metal's rough seams. The pin's weight is considerable — the investigator's wrist dips when lifting it one-handed.

[char:Nessa Tallow] tells the investigator: 'When I found him his collar was twisted half-round and the top button had popped clean off. I noticed it because it looked so wrong on a man who dressed so neat.'
clues.red_herring_explanation: The disturbed collar resulted from the violent fall against the wall, not strangulation. It misleads players toward strangulation.

clues.text: The victim's boots and the lower six inches of his trousers are crusted with dried salt and fine grit of the kind deposited by wave-wash on the shingle below the headland. His coat cuffs are similarly damp-stiffened.
clues.red_herring_explanation: Crayne walked the shingle beach regularly during his research and picked up salt residue on an earlier visit. It misleads players toward drowning.

clues.text: [char:Reverend Silas Crowe] tells the investigator: 'Mr Crayne came to evening prayers twice last week. On the second occasion he looked quite ill — pale, glassy-eyed. He told me his stomach had been troubling him for days.'
clues.red_herring_explanation: Crayne had a minor stomach complaint from poor diet during his stay. It misleads players toward poisoning.

clues.text: The ground inside the [loc:salt_store] doorway shows two sets of boot-prints in the fine salt-dust on the floor — one set belonging to the body's position, a second set facing them at close range and then retreating toward the door. The marks stop abruptly at the threshold.

[char:Nessa Tallow] tells the investigator: 'The door to the salt-store was pulled to but not latched when I arrived — I noticed because we always latch it against the wind. He was right there inside, up against the far wall. I didn't move him or touch a thing.'

On the compacted chalk of the [loc:headland_path], roughly halfway between the lane junction and the salt-store, a dark patch the size of a hand stains the ground. Two boot-scrapes cut through the chalk surface on either side of it.
clues.red_herring_explanation: The dark patch is tar from a barrel that a fisherman dragged along the path earlier that week; the scrapes are from the barrel's metal band. It misleads players toward the headland path as the murder site.

clues.text: In room four of [loc:anchor_inn], the writing desk's leather surface shows a fresh scuff running diagonally across it — the kind left by a heavy object dragged at speed — and the ink-well has been knocked sideways and not righted.
clues.red_herring_explanation: Crayne himself scuffed the desk and knocked the ink-well the previous afternoon in a hurry to leave for his meeting. It misleads players toward the inn as the murder site.

clues.text: [char:Pasco Tregidden] tells the investigator: 'That Crayne came poking around the [loc:harbour_office] twice in his first week here. Asking about old ledgers, old names. Mr Voss had words with him the second time and he didn't come back.'
clues.red_herring_explanation: Crayne's visits were genuine antiquarian research into old harbour records. Tregidden's framing of the confrontation exaggerates it. It misleads players toward the harbour office as a site of conflict and possible violence.

clues.answer: between ten and eleven o'clock at night
clues.text: [char:Miriam Quist] tells the investigator: 'I was at my window — I couldn't sleep. The church clock had just struck the half-hour after ten. I saw a lantern moving on the path up to the headland, going away from the village. A man carrying it, walking with a catch in his step.'

clues.answer: between ten and eleven o'clock at night
clues.text: [char:Nessa Tallow] tells the investigator: 'I walk that path every morning before light to check the pots. I went past the salt-store at [time:half five in the morning] — the body was cold and stiffened through. By first light he'd been lying there a good many hours.'

clues.answer: between eight and nine o'clock at night
clues.text: [char:Reverend Silas Crowe] tells the investigator: 'I saw Mr Crayne leaving the inn around [time:a quarter to eight in the evening] on Wednesday. He had his coat and hat on — dressed for being outdoors. I assumed he was going for his evening walk.'
clues.red_herring_explanation: Crayne left early and walked the village for over two hours before going to the salt-store. This sighting misleads players toward an earlier time of death.

clues.answer: between midnight and one o'clock in the morning
clues.text: [char:Dorothea Penrose] tells the investigator: 'I keep the light turning through the night. Just before [time:one o'clock in the morning] I thought I heard voices carrying on the wind from the direction of the headland — two men, though I couldn't make out words.'
clues.red_herring_explanation: Dorothea heard fishermen from a night-boat calling to one another near the cove far below — not voices from the salt-store. It misleads players toward a later time of death.

clues.answer: between two and three o'clock in the morning
clues.text: Inside the [loc:salt_store], a tallow candle stub on the window ledge has burned entirely down to a flat pool of wax; a candle of that size, fully intact, burns for approximately four to five hours.
clues.red_herring_explanation: The candle was already half-burned when Crayne arrived and lit it. It misleads players toward a much later time of death if they assume it was lit at the start of the encounter.

clues.text: Cross-referencing the stamp on the mooring pin with the ledger at [loc:harbour_office]: the octagonal-section iron pin pattern with a Spirewick harbour mark is recorded as exclusive to the harbourmaster's personal dock store — twelve issued, twelve listed as held there. The register is signed throughout by [char:Edmund Voss].

Asked to describe the figure she saw in more detail, [char:Miriam Quist] tells the investigator: 'He walked with a hitch — not a limp exactly, more like the right leg didn't straighten fully at the knee. I've seen that gait before, on the quay. It's particular.'

[char:Pasco Tregidden] tells the investigator: 'That Tallow woman had a blazing row with Crayne not a fortnight ago, right on the quay. She told him she'd see him off the headland herself if he came near her boats again. Half the village heard it.'
clues.red_herring_explanation: The dispute was a property disagreement that Nessa Tallow resolved by refusing the sale outright. The threatening phrasing was colloquial anger, not literal. It misleads players toward Nessa Tallow.

clues.text: [char:Pasco Tregidden] tells the investigator: 'I don't know why you'd need to look at those ledgers. They're all in order. Nobody needs to go through those.' He moves to stand between the investigator and the shelf holding the harbour fee records.
clues.red_herring_explanation: Tregidden is concealing petty theft from the harbour fees, not the murder. His evasiveness and attempt to block access to the ledgers is genuine but unrelated to Crayne's death. It misleads players toward Tregidden.

clues.text: In the vestry of [loc:church], a pair of heavy iron candlestick holders are stored upright in an open cupboard; one is missing, the vacant space in the wood still showing the circle of worn polish where it habitually stood.
clues.red_herring_explanation: The candlestick was lent to a parishioner for a home altar three weeks prior and has not yet been returned — it has no connection to the murder. It misleads players toward Reverend Crowe as the person who used a heavy implement.

clues.answer: preventing exposure of a false identity
clues.text: [char:Reverend Silas Crowe] tells the investigator: 'Mr Crayne came to me privately three days before he died. He asked me — quite carefully, as a philosophical matter — whether a man who had taken another man's name to survive was bound to confess that sin, or whether silence was its own mercy.'

clues.answer: preventing exposure of a false identity
clues.text: The landlord of [loc:anchor_inn], present in the room, tells the investigator: 'He carried that notebook everywhere — I never saw him without it. He kept it on his person, not in his room. But it wasn't on him when they brought the news, and it's not here either.'

[char:Pasco Tregidden] tells the investigator: 'Crayne was asking questions about the harbour accounts — when fees were collected, who kept the records. I told him that was none of his business and to speak to Mr Voss if he had questions.'
clues.red_herring_explanation: Crayne asked about the harbour accounts as part of his cover story of researching village history; he had no interest in current fee records. Tregidden's sensitivity is due to his own theft. It misleads players toward theft of harbour funds as the motive.

clues.text: [char:Nessa Tallow] tells the investigator: 'He offered me money for the boat — good money, more than it's worth. I said no. He came back twice more and each time the offer was a bit lower. I told him the third time that if he came back again I'd report him to the harbourmaster for harassment.'
clues.red_herring_explanation: Crayne wanted a boat for excursions to collect coastal research specimens — a mundane interest. The persistence was annoying but not sinister. It misleads players toward a property dispute as motive.

clues.answer: protecting a family secret about inheritance
clues.text: [char:Dorothea Penrose] tells the investigator: 'Mr Crayne asked me about the Penrose family twice — specifically about my father's brother, who emigrated before the war. He seemed very interested in whether my uncle had left a will and whether my father had ever challenged it.'
clues.red_herring_explanation: Crayne was researching local family histories as antiquarian cover for his stay. The Penrose inheritance questions were a dead end he'd already abandoned. It misleads players toward a family inheritance secret as motive.

leads.text: A fisherman on the quay mentions that [char:Nessa Tallow] found the body at [loc:salt_store] and is still there, waiting to speak to whoever is in charge.

[char:Miriam Quist] at [loc:quist_cottage] is known in the village as someone who rarely sleeps and watches the lane at all hours — the constable's note says she may have seen something on Wednesday night.
The victim lodged at [loc:anchor_inn] for three weeks and kept a room there; his belongings have not been touched since the news arrived.

opening_narrative:
The coastal village of Spirewick sits at the foot of two pale limestone spires where a narrow inlet cuts into the Cornish cliffs. By [time:mid-November] the tourist trade is long gone, the herring boats are hauled up, and the village draws inward around its coal fires. It was into this quiet that [char:Aldous Crayne] arrived three weeks ago — a polite, private man with a leather satchel and a notebook, who told the landlord of [loc:anchor_inn] he was writing a monograph on pre-Norman salt-working.

On the morning of Thursday the fifteenth, [char:Nessa Tallow] walked the [loc:headland_path] before dawn as she did every day to check her family's crab pots near the [loc:salt_store]. She found the door pulled to and a man dead inside. The village constable, out of his depth, sent word to the county office before [time:eight o'clock in the morning]. By [time:noon] it was clear that no one in Spirewick was speaking freely to anyone they knew.

The investigator arrives at [loc:anchor_inn] by the [time:early afternoon], the last light of a grey November day already beginning to fail over the sea. The landlord has kept the fire going in the public room and has a list of names ready — people the constable suggested might know something. The spires of pale rock bracket the inlet on either side, and the wind off the water smells of salt and coming rain.
